children.value: Standard HMC preconditioning adapts the mass matrix to match the posterior geometry:
default): adapts a per-parameter scale via Welford’s variance estimator. Efficient and robust but ignores correlations.
): full covariance estimate via Welford’s algorithm. Captures all correlations but is

children.html: O(D2)O(D^2)O(D2)

children.value: in memory/compute and can overfit when warmup samples are few.

children.value: correction to a diagonal mass matrix by
— the expected squared norm of the difference between the score of the preconditioned distribution and a standard normal. The inverse mass matrix has the form

children.html: M−1=diag⁡(σ)(I+U(Λ−I)U⊤)diag⁡(σ)M^{-1} = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma)\bigl(I + U(\Lambda - I)U^\top\bigr)\operatorname{diag}(\sigma)M−1=diag(σ)(I+U(Λ−I)U⊤)diag(σ)
σ∈R>0d\sigma \in \mathbb{R}^d_{>0}σ∈R>0d​
U∈Rd×kU \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}U∈Rd×k
Λ=diag⁡(λ)\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda)Λ=diag(λ)
k≪dk \ll dk≪d
O(dk)O(dk)O(dk)

children.value: , so this scales to high dimensions while still capturing the dominant correlations.
The key insight is that the optimal diagonal scale from the Fisher criterion is

children.html: σi∗=(Var⁡[xi]Var⁡[∇ilog⁡p(x)])1/4\sigma_i^* = \left(\frac{\operatorname{Var}[x_i]}{\operatorname{Var}[\nabla_i \log p(x)]}\right)^{1/4}σi∗​=(Var[∇i​logp(x)]Var[xi​]​)1/4

children.value: which uses both the curvature (gradient variance) and the spread (sample variance), giving a more statistically efficient estimator than Welford’s variance alone.

children.value: Helper: run NUTS with each adaptation scheme

children.label: Helper: run NUTS with each adaptation scheme

children.value: We define a single helper that runs NUTS warmup with a chosen adaptation strategy, then draws posterior samples across multiple independent chains. The function returns the samples and a count of gradient evaluations (including those during warmup), so we can compare efficiency fairly.

def run_nuts(logdensity_fn, init_params, rng_key, *,
             adaptation="diag", num_warmup=500, num_samples=1000,
             num_chains=4, max_rank=10):
    """Run NUTS with multiple chains in parallel via jax.vmap.

    Each chain runs its own warmup and sampling. All chains are vmapped
    so they execute simultaneously on the same device.

    Parameters
    ----------
    adaptation : {"diag", "dense", "low_rank"}
    max_rank   : only used when adaptation="low_rank"

    Returns
    -------
    samples : pytree with shape (num_chains, num_samples, ...)
    n_grad  : total gradient evaluations across all chains (warmup + sampling)
    """
    if adaptation == "low_rank":
        warmup = blackjax.low_rank_window_adaptation(
            blackjax.nuts, logdensity_fn, max_rank=max_rank
        )
    else:
        warmup = blackjax.window_adaptation(
            blackjax.nuts, logdensity_fn,
            is_mass_matrix_diagonal=(adaptation == "diag"),
        )

    def one_chain(rng_key):
        k_warm, k_sample = jax.random.split(rng_key)
        (state, params), warmup_info = warmup.run(k_warm, init_params, num_warmup)
        warmup_grads = jnp.sum(warmup_info.info.num_integration_steps)

        nuts = blackjax.nuts(logdensity_fn, **params)

        def step(state, key):
            state, info = nuts.step(key, state)
            return state, (state.position, info.num_integration_steps)

        keys = jax.random.split(k_sample, num_samples)
        _, (samples, n_steps) = jax.lax.scan(step, state, keys)
        return samples, warmup_grads + jnp.sum(n_steps)

    chain_keys = jax.random.split(rng_key, num_chains)
    samples, all_grads = jax.vmap(one_chain)(chain_keys)
    return samples, int(jnp.sum(all_grads))
Example 1 — Correlated Gaussian with low-rank structure

children.label: Example 1 — Correlated Gaussian with low-rank structure

children.html: D=50D = 50D=50

children.value: dimensional Gaussian whose covariance matrix has

children.html: k=3k = 3k=3

children.value: dominant directions (eigenvalues 100, 50, 20) and unit variance in the remaining 47 directions. This is the canonical setting where the low-rank mass matrix should clearly outperform the diagonal (which misses correlations) and match or exceed the dense mass matrix (which must estimate 1275 covariance entries from warmup samples alone).

D = 50
k_true = 3

rng_key, k_cov = jax.random.split(rng_key)

# Random orthonormal basis for the low-rank directions
Q, _ = jnp.linalg.qr(jax.random.normal(k_cov, (D, k_true)))
true_eigs = jnp.array([100.0, 50.0, 20.0])

# Covariance = Q diag(λ) Qᵀ + I  (remaining directions have variance 1)
cov = Q @ jnp.diag(true_eigs) @ Q.T + jnp.eye(D)
cov_inv = jnp.linalg.inv(cov)

def gauss_logdensity(params):
    x = params["x"]
    return -0.5 * x @ cov_inv @ x

init_gauss = {"x": jnp.zeros(D)}

rng_key, k_diag, k_dense, k_lr = jax.random.split(rng_key, 4)

gauss_diag,  ngrad_gauss_diag  = run_nuts(gauss_logdensity, init_gauss, k_diag,  adaptation="diag")
gauss_dense, ngrad_gauss_dense = run_nuts(gauss_logdensity, init_gauss, k_dense, adaptation="dense")
gauss_lr,    ngrad_gauss_lr    = run_nuts(gauss_logdensity, init_gauss, k_lr,    adaptation="low_rank")
Effective Sample Size per gradient evaluation

children.label: Effective Sample Size per gradient evaluation

children.value: A fair comparison accounts for the fact that NUTS takes variable numbers of leapfrog steps per sample. ESS/gradient-eval measures how many effective samples we get per unit of compute.

theta_grid = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)

ax.set_xlabel("PC 1")
ax.set_ylabel("PC 2")
ax.set_title("Samples projected onto top-2 eigenvectors")
ax.legend(fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()

rng_key, k_data = jax.random.split(rng_key)
n_students, n_items = 100, 20
k1, k2, k3, k4 = jax.random.split(k_data, 4)

true_theta = jax.random.normal(k1, (n_students,))
true_b     = jax.random.normal(k2, (n_items,))
true_a     = jnp.abs(jax.random.normal(k3, (n_items,))) + 0.5
logits     = true_a[None, :] * (true_theta[:, None] - true_b[None, :])
responses  = jax.random.bernoulli(k4, jax.nn.sigmoid(logits)).astype(jnp.float64)
print(f"Response matrix: {responses.shape},  mean correct: {responses.mean():.2f}")

rng_key, k_diag, k_dense, k_lr = jax.random.split(rng_key, 4)

irt_diag,  ngrad_irt_diag  = run_nuts(irt_logdensity, init_params_irt, k_diag,  adaptation="diag")
irt_dense, ngrad_irt_dense = run_nuts(irt_logdensity, init_params_irt, k_dense, adaptation="dense")
irt_lr,    ngrad_irt_lr    = run_nuts(irt_logdensity, init_params_irt, k_lr,    adaptation="low_rank")

children.value: , giving the low-rank mass matrix a clear rank-2 structure to exploit. However, the centered parameterization mixes much more slowly and takes significantly longer to run, which is why we use the flat noncentred model here.
The Correlated Gaussian example above cleanly isolates the benefit of the low-rank metric without this confound.

The low-rank mass matrix adaptation combines the scalability of the diagonal estimator with much of the correlation-capture ability of the dense estimator. The Fisher-divergence objective uses both the gradient variance and sample variance, giving a more efficient estimator than Welford’s algorithm alone. Because only

children.html: k≪Dk \ll Dk≪D

children.value: eigenvectors are stored, it remains practical at hundreds of dimensions where dense mass matrices become expensive.

works well across many posteriordb models. For posteriors with a known low-dimensional structure (e.g. an IRT model where a small number of item factors drive student ability) you can increase it; for very high-dimensional posteriors (

children.html: D>103D > 10^3D>103

children.value: ) you may want to reduce it to keep per-step cost low.

children.html: [1/cutoff,cutoff][1/\text{cutoff}, \text{cutoff}][1/cutoff,cutoff]

children.value: are masked to 1 (no-op correction). The default
avoids applying corrections for directions that are nearly already isotropic after diagonal scaling.
